title: The CEO Email
hardTitle: i think i got scammed?? please dont tell mike
blurb: "I think I got scammed." The CEO asked for gift cards. It wasn't the CEO.
hardBlurb: tanya from copper kettle. something about gift cards for the CEO. she sounds shaky.

n1.text:
Tanya from Copper Kettle's office calls, talking fast and quiet:

"I think I did something really stupid. Mike — the CEO — emailed me this morning asking me to buy gift cards for a client thank-you. Said it was urgent and confidential. I bought four hundred dollars' worth at lunch, and I was about to email him the codes when something felt... off. Am I in trouble?"
n1.hint: Two jobs in your first sentence: stop the money from moving, and keep the reporter feeling safe for having called.

choices.label: "You are not in trouble — calling us was exactly right. Do NOT send those codes to anyone. Now walk me through it: forward me that email exactly as you got it."
choices.feedback: First words matter: stop the loss, kill the shame. These scams are built by professionals to work on smart people — and an employee who feels safe reporting is worth more than any filter you will ever deploy.

choices.label: "Forward me the email and I'll take a look."
choices.feedback: Right ask, missing two things: an explicit "don't send the codes" — she was minutes from doing it — and a word of reassurance for someone who just asked if she's in trouble. Security incidents are people incidents first.

choices.label: "You almost emailed gift card codes to a stranger? Didn't you take the security training last quarter?"
choices.feedback: Congratulations: Tanya will never report anything again, and neither will anyone she tells about this call. Shame doesn't prevent the next incident — it just guarantees you hear about it later, after the money is gone.

n2.text:
The email lands in your inbox. From: "Mike Harrelson <mharrelson@copperkettIe-group.com>" — a capital I where the l should be. Urgent tone, "keep this between us," and "I'm in meetings all day, don't call."

Classic. The real question is how big this is.
n2.hint: That's one email you know about. How do you find out how many you don't?

choices.label: Run a message trace: who else at Copper Kettle got mail from that lookalike domain?
choices.feedback: Phishing is a campaign, not a letter. Tanya is the one who called — she's rarely the only one who received it. Scope first, exactly like an outage: you can't contain what you haven't measured.

choices.label: Block the sender address and tell Tanya she's all set.
choices.feedback: Blocking one address treats the symptom you can see. Attackers rotate senders in minutes — and you still have no idea who else got the email or answered it. Contain after you scope, not instead of scoping.

choices.label: Reply to the email to string the scammer along and waste their time.
choices.feedback: You just confirmed the mailbox is live and monitored, on company time, mid-incident. This isn't a hobby duel — every minute spent entertaining the attacker is a minute Devon in accounting might be buying cards.

n3.text:
The trace comes back ugly: six people received it. Five ignored it. Devon in accounts payable replied "Sure — which cards and how many?" twenty minutes ago.

And Tanya adds one more thing: "Monday I got a 'purchase approval' link from Mike and signed in with my email password. Was that... part of this?"
n3.hint: A phished password means someone may already be inside the mailbox — and a reset alone does not end sessions that are already open. Also: someone else is mid-mistake right now.

choices.label: Treat Tanya's account as compromised: reset the password, revoke every active session, check her mailbox rules — and call Devon RIGHT NOW before he buys anything.
choices.feedback: Phished credentials mean the attacker may be IN the mailbox now — a reset alone doesn't end sessions that are already open, and mailbox rules survive password changes. And Devon is an active bleed: people first, forensics second.

choices.label: Reset Tanya's password and move on to cleanup.
choices.feedback: Necessary but not sufficient. Existing sessions keep working after a reset unless you revoke them, inbox rules persist, and Devon is still typing. A half-contained account compromise isn't contained.

choices.label: Kick off a full antivirus scan on Tanya's PC.
choices.feedback: Nothing was installed — her password was harvested on a fake login page. The scan will come back clean, you'll feel productive, and the attacker will still be logged into her mailbox reading everything. Match the response to the attack.

n4.text:
Devon caught in time — he was literally in line at the store. Then you open Tanya's mailbox rules and there it is: created Monday, forwards anything containing "invoice," "payment," or "gift" to an outside Gmail address, marks it read.

That's how the fake Mike knew exactly how to sound like the real one.
n4.hint: Before you clean anything up, think about who will later need proof of what happened — the insurer, the bank, the boss.

choices.label: Remove the rule, block the lookalike domain, purge the phish from all six mailboxes — and preserve the evidence, then brief the real Mike: this is a reportable incident, not just a bad email.
choices.feedback: Contain, clean, and document — in that order. Screenshots of the rule and the message headers are what the insurer, the bank, and possibly law enforcement will ask for. And the CEO learns about his impersonation from you, today, not from a client next month.

choices.label: Delete the rule, block the domain, close the ticket quietly.
choices.feedback: The technical cleanup is right — but a week of silently forwarded financial email is an incident leadership needs to know about, in writing. If money or client data moved and it surfaces later, "IT knew and closed the ticket" is a very bad sentence.

choices.label: Delete the rule and every trace of the phishing emails so nobody clicks them later.
choices.feedback: You just shredded the evidence. Headers, the rule, the sent replies — that's the forensic record of what the attacker saw and did. Purge malicious mail from inboxes, yes, but preserve copies first. "Clean" and "gone" are different goals.

n5.text:
By 16:30 it's contained: account secured, rule documented and removed, domain blocked, Mike briefed, and the store refunded the unactivated cards.

Tanya, one more time: "I still feel so stupid. I talk to Mike every day. How did I not know?"
n5.hint: She caught what the spam filter missed. What do you want her — and everyone she talks to — to do next time?

choices.label: "That email fooled the spam filter too, and it was built from a week of reading real messages. You're the one who caught it — reporting it IS the win. Mind if I use it, no names, for a two-minute heads-up to the staff?"
choices.feedback: Reframe the reporter as the hero, because she is — her gut feeling beat the filter. And a real, local example (anonymized) teaches the tells better than any generic training slide ever will.

choices.label: "Don't worry about it — it's all fixed now."
choices.feedback: Kind, but it closes the door on the teachable moment. She asked HOW she missed it — that's an invitation to show her the tells: urgency, secrecy, a payment method no CEO uses, and a domain one letter off.

choices.label: "Just double-check the sender address next time."
choices.feedback: That's blame wearing a helpful hat — and bad advice besides: the whole point of a lookalike domain is that "double-checking" fails at a glance. She needs the pattern (urgency + secrecy + weird payment), not a chore.

n6.text:
Incident notes: lookalike-domain BEC targeting six staff. Credential phish Monday → forwarding rule → tailored gift-card fraud. Contained same day: sessions revoked, rule preserved then removed, domain blocked, zero dollars lost. Leadership briefed; staff awareness note approved.

Mike's reply: "Glad someone was paying attention. Buy Tanya lunch on the company card."

Ticket closed.
